reason: Confirmed product requirements, scope boundaries, acceptance examples, and reference rationale for M3.
M3 depends on M1 plugin host, capability registry, and hook kernel behavior.
M3 depends on M2 provider bridge returning tool intent without executing tools.
Cross-project tool registry, permission, hook, and result handling patterns to ground implementation choices.
Agent loop implications for tool scheduling, failure return, path conflict handling, and message pairing.
Entry point for backend implementation guidelines relevant to core runtime work.
Error handling patterns are directly relevant to structured tool failures and permission denials.
Quality expectations for backend/core TypeScript changes.
M3 changes contracts spanning provider bridge, core loop, plugins, tests, and future host adapters.
Execution pipeline and permission runtime should reuse existing contract/event/hook patterns where possible.
